description_long: This object is a stylized, reddish-brown torii gate, commonly found at the entrance to Shinto shrines in Japan. It features two vertical posts supporting two horizontal crossbars, with the upper crossbar slightly curved upward and extending beyond the posts. The structure is simple and robust, with a rectangular block at the center of the upper crossbar, possibly representing a decorative or structural element. The surface appears to be smooth and matte, with no visible electronic components, screens, or control interfaces. The object is likely constructed from wood or a wood-like material, with a painted or coated finish. Its design is iconic and symbolic, serving as a cultural marker rather than a functional electronic device.
description: A reddish-brown torii gate with two vertical posts and two horizontal crossbars, featuring a curved top bar and a central rectangular block.
description_view_0: Front view showing the full structure, including both vertical posts, the curved top crossbar, and the central rectangular block.
description_view_1: Side view revealing the thin profile of the gate and the depth of the crossbars.
description_view_2: Rear view similar to the front, showing the back of the posts and crossbars, with less detail on the central block.
description_view_3: Opposite side view, again highlighting the slim profile and the alignment of the posts and crossbars.
